content:
First we work in a convenient coordinate‐system.  Let

A=(0,0),  B=(5,0),  D=(0,12),  C=(5,12).

We are told that E has perpendicular distance 12 from the line AB (the x–axis) and distance 1 from AD (the y–axis), and that ΔABE is acute.  The only choice that puts E “below” AB at distance 12 and at x–distance 1 from AD, and makes ΔABE acute, is

E=(1,−12).

Now we seek a line through E which cuts the 5×12 rectangle into two regions of equal area (=30 each), meeting AB at F and CD at G.  Any such line through E has equation

y +12 = m(x−1),  

so in slope–intercept form  
  y = m(x−1) −12.

It meets AB (y=0) at  
  0 = m(x_F−1) −12  ⇒  x_F = 1 + 12/m,

and meets CD (y=12) at  
  12 = m(x_G−1) −12  ⇒  x_G = 1 + 24/m.

One checks that for m>6 these intersections lie on the sides AB and CD respectively (with 0≤x_F<x_G≤5).  A quick “slice‐by‐slice” area computation shows that the area of the portion of the rectangle lying below this line is

A(m) = 48 − 216/m.

Setting A(m)=30 gives

48 − 216/m =30   ⇒   216/m =18   ⇒   m=12.

Thus the cutting line is

y +12 = 12(x−1)   ⇒   y =12x −24.

Hence

F=(x_F,0)=(1+12/12,0)=(2,0),  
G=(x_G,12)=(1+24/12,12)=(3,12).

Finally

FG² = (3−2)² + (12−0)² = 1 +144 =145.

Answer: \boxed{145}.
